narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your undergrad and LSAT prep—it's tough to hear that your law school application didn't make the cut because our model sees a 55.3% chance you might not pass the bar exam down the line. Picture this: the model looked at your story as a whole, like piecing together chapters of a book that, for many similar students, has led to struggles at the bar. Your LSAT score of 39 stood out as the biggest red flag, the strongest pull toward that risk, because it signals to the model that tackling the intense bar material could be an uphill battle right from the start. Then came your Year 1 decile of 2, right there in second place as a major influence, showing early law school grades that clustered you with others who've historically faltered. Your Year 3 decile of 2 echoed that pattern, reinforcing the sense that consistency in a demanding program might be challenging. Layered on top was your middle family income bracket, which the model associates with added pressures that have tripped up others in similar spots. Even your solid undergrad GPA of 3.1, while respectable, nudged things further toward caution in the model's view, as it weighs how that foundation holds up against bar-level rigor. Your background as a white female full-time student rounded out the picture, each subtly tipping the scales based on patterns from thousands of past journeys. It's not that any one piece dooms you—plenty of folks beat these odds—but together, they painted a profile the model sees as riskier than we'd like for admission. This isn't the end of your story; think of it as a nudge to boost that LSAT or build stronger early grades elsewhere. You've got the drive—use this to rewrite the next chapter. We're rooting for you.
